skill.name: baoyu-post-to-wechat
skill.description: Post Markdown articles and image-text drafts to WeChat Official Account using Chrome automation, clipboard transfer, and WeChat-ready HTML rendering.
skill.summary: Convert Markdown into WeChat-ready drafts and automate posting through Chrome.
skill.author: JimLiu

security_audit.summary: Manual review confirms the skill is designed for WeChat publishing, not confirmed malware, and no prompt injection attempt was found. However, it uses shell-based browser automation, Chrome DevTools control, persistent login profiles, clipboard manipulation, filesystem access, and remote content fetching. These confirmed risks require human review before marketplace publication.

high_findings.title: Shell command construction in browser automation
high_findings.description: The alternate agent-browser script builds a command string for execSync and later passes user-derived title and content into eval-style browser commands. This confirms a real command-injection risk if untrusted text reaches this path, although the behavior appears intended for WeChat browser automation rather than malicious execution.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct execSync usage with a composed shell command is present, and later calls build eval commands from runtime content. The surrounding script purpose is legitimate automation, so this is high risk but not confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Chrome DevTools automation with persistent authenticated profile
high_findings.description: The skill launches Chrome with a remote debugging port and a persistent profile used for WeChat login, then drives the session through CDP. This is legitimate for the stated posting workflow, but it exposes a powerful local browser-control surface and retained account state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The remote debugging flags, WebSocket connection, and persistent profile are explicit. Risk depends on local host trust and runtime permissions, so confidence is high but not critical.

high_findings.title: System clipboard and global keystroke automation
high_findings.description: The scripts invoke osascript, xdotool, ydotool, PowerShell, Swift, and clipboard tools to copy content and send paste keystrokes to the active application. This is expected for rich WeChat editor pasting, but it can overwrite clipboard data or affect the wrong foreground app.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct and intentionally control OS clipboard and paste actions. The risk is confirmed, while the implementation context supports a legitimate publishing use case.

medium_findings.title: Remote content fetching from Markdown inputs
medium_findings.description: Markdown image URLs are downloaded over HTTP or HTTPS into a temporary directory, and PlantUML diagrams can be sent to an external rendering server. This can disclose document content or fetch untrusted remote resources during article conversion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network fetches are explicit and content is derived from local Markdown. The risk is credible, but it is a feature path and no credential exfiltration endpoint was found.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized HTML generation in Markdown extensions
medium_findings.description: Several Markdown extensions interpolate token text, rendered SVG, or error text directly into HTML. Because the generated HTML is opened locally and pasted into WeChat, untrusted Markdown could produce unsafe markup or unexpected editor content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The interpolation is visible in renderer code, but exploitation depends on whether the Markdown source is trusted and how the generated HTML is consumed. This warrants review rather than a block.

low_findings.title: Limited environment-variable access for local configuration
low_findings.description: Environment access is limited to selecting the Chrome executable and profile base directory. I did not find evidence that secrets are read or sent over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced environment variables are visible and configuration-oriented. No credential variable names or exfiltration flow were found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static scanner false positives in documentation and renderer code
low_findings.description: Many reported Ruby backtick, weak-crypto, network-scanning, keylogger, and C2 hits are false positives from Markdown fences, SVG path data, CSS names, parser regexes, or non-security encoding. Examples include documentation command blocks, PlantUML encoding, SVG icons, and an md5 hash used only for temporary image cache names.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed these locations are examples, SVG literals, encoding helpers, or cache identifiers. They do not perform the malicious behavior named by the static pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command construction in browser automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The alternate agent-browser script builds a command string for execSync and later passes user-derived title and content into eval-style browser commands. This confirms a real command-injection risk if untrusted text reaches this path, although the behavior appears intended for WeChat browser automation rather than malicious execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct execSync usage with a composed shell command is present, and later calls build eval commands from runtime content. The surrounding script purpose is legitimate automation, so this is high risk but not confirmed malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Publishing polished WeChat drafts from Markdown requires formatting, images, and browser steps. This skill automates conversion, clipboard transfer, and WeChat editor population.
actual_capabilities: Converts Markdown articles into WeChat-ready HTML with selectable themes.
Extracts titles, authors, summaries, and image placeholders from Markdown frontmatter and body text.
Launches Chrome and automates WeChat Official Account editor actions through CDP.
Uploads image-text post images from files or sorted image directories.
Copies rich HTML and images to the system clipboard for pasting into the WeChat editor.
Supports draft saving after the user has logged in to WeChat in the opened browser.
limitations: Requires Chrome, Bun through npx, and local OS clipboard permissions.
Requires manual WeChat login by QR code before posting can continue.
WeChat page changes can break selectors and automation steps.
The automation has elevated local permissions and needs security review before marketplace use.

use_cases.title: Publish Markdown Articles
use_cases.description: Convert a prepared Markdown article into a formatted WeChat Official Account draft with title, author, summary, and inline images.

use_cases.title: Prepare Image-Text Posts
use_cases.description: Create a WeChat image-text post from a short Markdown source and a folder of ordered images.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Publishing
use_cases.description: Use shared Markdown structure and themes so team articles follow a repeatable WeChat publishing workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Post a Simple Draft
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for one Markdown article.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use baoyu-post-to-wechat to prepare a WeChat draft from my Markdown file. Keep the browser open for my review before saving.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Image-Text Post
prompt_templates.scenario: Use a Markdown file and an image directory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a WeChat image-text post from the Markdown file and the images folder. Compress the title and content if needed.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Theme and Summary
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate article workflow with metadata control.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my Markdown article with the grace theme, use the frontmatter author, generate the summary, and open the WeChat editor for review.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Before Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Before using the WeChat automation, inspect the Markdown for remote images, scripts, and unexpected HTML. Then prepare the draft without final submission.

output_examples.input: A Markdown article with frontmatter, headings, paragraphs, and three local images.
output_examples.output: A WeChat draft opens in Chrome with formatted article content and the three images inserted at their placeholders.

output_examples.input: A short Markdown note and a folder containing numbered image files.
output_examples.output: A WeChat image-text draft is populated with a compressed title, concise content, and the sorted images.

output_examples.input: A Markdown article that references a remote image URL.
output_examples.output: The remote image is downloaded to a temporary local file before the draft workflow inserts it into the editor.

best_practices: Use trusted local Markdown files and review remote image URLs before running the automation.
Run the skill in a dedicated Chrome profile that is used only for WeChat publishing.
Keep final submission manual unless the draft has been reviewed in the WeChat editor.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill on untrusted Markdown that may contain unexpected HTML or remote resources.
Do not use a primary browser profile that contains unrelated sensitive sessions.
Do not leave the Chrome debugging session open longer than needed for publishing.

faq.question: Does this skill publish directly to WeChat?
faq.answer: It can save a draft through browser automation, but the safer workflow is to review the draft before final submission.

faq.question: What tools must be installed locally?
faq.answer: Chrome and Bun through npx are required. Clipboard helper tools may be required on Linux or Windows.

faq.question: Does it require WeChat credentials in environment variables?
faq.answer: No evidence found for credential environment variables. Login is handled through the opened browser session.

faq.question: Can it handle images in Markdown?
faq.answer: Yes. It replaces image references with placeholders, then inserts local or downloaded images into the WeChat editor.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It controls Chrome through CDP, runs local commands, manipulates the clipboard, and keeps an authenticated browser profile.

faq.question: Should it be used with untrusted content?
faq.answer: No. Review Markdown, HTML, and remote image links before running the automation.
